subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear narrative structure with smooth transitions between introduction, mathematical background, modelling, calculations, and conclusion
Consistent use of headings, numbered sections, tables, figures, page numbers and labels to aid navigation
Effective visuals (photograph of vase, rightâangled triangle diagram, overhead image confirming circular crossâsection)
Descriptive title and cover image grounding the exploration in a real object
weaknesses: Minor transcription errors in tables (Table 2 repeating a function, misâcopied volume value) interrupt the flow
Aim statement ends abruptly and the appendix is not referenced in the main text
Some derivations and price calculations are overly lengthy and only loosely connected to the aim, reducing conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands (coherence and organization) sit firmly in band 3, with effective logical development and structure. Conciseness is closer to band 2 due to unnecessary material. Overall, the criterion aligns with a band 3 performance.

strengths: Largely correct and appropriate mathematical notation (functions, integrals, limits, units) with only minor slips
Effective use of multiple representations (tables, 2-D graphs, 3-D GeoGebra renderings, photographs)
Logical progression of explanations accompanying algebraic work and GDC outputs
weaknesses: Inconsistent unit formatting and spacing (e.g., â65m^2/5lâ)
Occasional abrupt jumps (skipping intermediate steps in surface-area integral)
Generic explanations (Riemann sums) not explicitly tied to the vase context, and deferred coordinates in the appendix reduce clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands (language/notation, representations, logical clarity) perform at band 3, showing effective but not fully consistent or exceptional mathematical communication.

strengths: Choosing a personally owned vase and collecting own measurements demonstrates significant independent decisionâmaking
Investigation grounded in a personal hobby with autonomous selection of measurement techniques and software
Use of image superimposition, Lagrange interpolation and RÂ² checks shows good initiative
weaknesses: Most mathematical procedures follow standard classroom exemplars, limiting evidence of outstanding independent thinking
No exploration of alternative modelling strategies (e.g., varying number of sections) to push creativity further
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand shows clear personal engagement and initiative at a âgoodâ rather than âexceptionalâ level, aligning with band 2.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on how results meet initial aims and discussion of measurement uncertainties
Thoughtful analysis of error sources with suggestions for improvement (e.g., Vernier callipers, coding)
Clear evaluation of outcomes with implications and potential extensions (wall thickness, experimental verification)
weaknesses: Reflection is confined mostly to the conclusion rather than woven throughout
Quantification of uncertainty (e.g., thread method error) is omitted
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Depth of reflection, critical analysis and evaluation consistently meet band 2 descriptorsâmeaningful but not deeply insightful or comprehensive.

strengths: Relevant calculus techniques (solids of revolution, surfaceâarea derivation) and Lagrange interpolation are correctly applied
Mathematics is fully commensurate with SL level and supports the task effectively
Thorough understanding demonstrated in derivatives, integrals and RÂ² calculation with sensible numerical results
weaknesses: Minor computational and transcription slips (duplicate functions, small table errors)
Some steps (justification of approximations, specification of integral limits) are not fully explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands exhibit ârelevant mathematics used effectivelyâ and âgood understanding with minor errors,â matching band 4 performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations usually accompany algebraic work and GDC outputs, enabling the reader to follow the logical progression from measurement to final results. Occasional abrupt jumps (e.g., surface-area integral evaluated only by GDC without intermediate working) reduce absolute clarity, keeping the strand in band 3.

Derivatives and integrals are largely accurate, the $R^{2}$ calculation is correct, and final numerical answers are sensible. A few transcription errors (duplicate green/pink function, small table error) indicate minor inaccuracies, so the strand fits âgood understanding and mostly correctâ rather than the flawless band 5 or 6.

The student employs tables, 2-D graphs, 3-D GeoGebra renderings and photographs to support the mathematics. Each representation is explained and connected to the text, demonstrating effective â though not outstanding â multiple representation use.

The report follows a clear narrative: introduction of the problem, mathematical background, modelling, calculations, and conclusion. Transitions between these sections are generally smooth and the reader can follow the argument without difficulty. Minor lapses (e.g., a repeated function in Table 2 and a mis-copied âblueâ volume value) interrupt the flow but do not derail it, thus fitting the descriptor of a coherent and logically developed piece rather than an âexcellentâ one.

Employing image superimposition, Lagrange interpolation and an $R^{2}$ check illustrates a good degree of initiative. Still, the mathematical creativity stops short of, for example, optimising the number of sections or testing alternative modelling strategies, so the work fits a âgoodâ rather than âexceptionalâ level.

Sources of error (thread flexibility, point selection, manual data entry) are identified and their likely effects outlined. The student suggests using Vernier callipers or coding to reduce error, demonstrating thoughtful â but not âdeep and insightfulâ â analysis.

Outcomes (cost â Â£0.11, water volume 2.127 l) are interpreted and the student comments on potential extensions (including wall thickness and experimental verification). This represents a clear evaluation with implications, aligning with band 2.

The conclusion discusses how the calculated paint volume and water capacity meet the initial aims and reflects on measurement uncertainties. The reflection is meaningful and threaded through the final section, but it does not permeate the whole report, so the depth meets, not exceeds, band 2.

Functions, integrals, limits and units are written with largely correct symbols; superscripts, differentials and Greek letters are used appropriately. A few slips (missing âÂ²â in 
$cm2$, inconsistent rounding in coefficients) prevent a fully consistent (band 4) rating.

The investigation is framed around a personal hobby (painting vases) and cost estimation, and the student selects measurement techniques and software autonomously. This constitutes a clear personal approach, albeit not highly innovative.

While most material is relevant to determining the vaseâs surface area and volume, lengthy derivations already available in textbooks (derivation of surface-area formula, full statement of Lagrange theorem) and very detailed price calculations inflate the length to 24 pages. These additions are only loosely connected to the aim, so conciseness is âmostlyâ rather than âfullyâ achieved.

Calculus of solids of revolution, derivation of surface-area formula, polynomial interpolation and regression are all directly relevant and properly applied to the problem. Although minor computational slips exist, the mathematics generally supports the task effectively, placing it in the ârelevant mathematics used effectivelyâ band.

Choosing a personally owned vase, collecting measurements, and deciding to split the outline into four separate interpolation curves show noticeable independent decisions beyond classroom exemplars. However, most mathematical procedures (solid of revolution, Lagrange interpolation) are standard, so evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

Headings, numbered sections, tables and figures are used consistently; the introduction, body and conclusion are all present and correctly ordered. Page numbers and labels aid navigation. However, some tables (Table 2 and Table 3) carry small transcription errors and the appendix is not referenced inside the text, so the organisation is good rather than exceptional.

The techniques employed (integration of cubic/quadratic functions, Lagrange interpolation up to cubic, numerical evaluation on a GDC) are solidly within HL syllabus expectations and are applied correctly. The work is thus fully commensurate with course level and used effectively, warranting band 4.

annotations.comment: The expression â65m^2/5lâ lacks units formatting and spacing. Recommend writing as $$65\,\mathrm{m}^2\,/\,5\,\mathrm{L}$$ for consistency.
The aim statement ends abruptly. Suggest completing the sentence to explicitly state the volume purpose, ensuring clarity of objectives.
The photograph of the vase in Figure 1 effectively grounds the exploration in a real object, supporting the mathematical modelling.
The right-angled triangle diagram is crisp and supports the Pythagorean derivation effectively.
The title is clear and descriptive, immediately conveying the explorationâs focus on vase analysis and calculus. This sets a coherent tone for the reader.
The cover image effectively contextualizes the vase under study, enhancing engagement and presenting a vivid real-world connection.
The introductory paragraph frames personal fascination well, demonstrating clear independent thinking and personal engagement with the topic.
When introducing $$V=\int_a^b\pi y^2\,dx$$, clarify the limits $a$ and $b$ in the vase context to guide the readerâs understanding.
The discussion of Riemann sums is generic; consider linking this explanation explicitly to approximating the vase volume to improve clarity.
The approximation $$A = 2\pi\bigl(\tfrac{f(x_{i-1})+f(x_i)}2\bigr)ds$$ skips justification for replacing both radii with $$f(x_i^*)$$. Suggest a brief rationale.
The arc-length paragraph is lengthy; include intermediate algebraic steps to demonstrate how $$ds=\sqrt{1+(dy/dx)^2}dx$$ is obtained.
The final surface-area formula is presented correctly and concisely, clearly linking geometric intuition with calculus.
The frustum diagram (Figure 3) is well-drawn, illustrating the transition from frustum to integral derivation very effectively.
The standalone fragment âtriangle.â appears disconnected. Merge with preceding sentence for clarity and logical flow.
The overhead image in Figure 6 confirms the vaseâs circular cross-section well, reinforcing the choice of solid-of-revolution models.
The description of circumference measurement omits quantification of uncertainty from the thread method. Consider estimating this error.
Before the graphing section, include a brief roadmap of the steps to improve structural coherence and guide the reader.
Table 1âs headers repeat âRadius at Point.â Renaming one column (e.g., âRadius at Point Bâ) would prevent confusion.
When calculating $$r=\frac{27.4}{2\pi}\approx4.361$$, include âcmâ units and discuss rounding uncertainty.
Stating the use of a TI-Inspire GDC tools is helpful; this transparency in mathematical communication aids reproducibility.
Coordinates are deferred to the appendix, but including a sample set in the main text would enhance clarity and reduce reader effort.
The GeoGebra footnote uses inconsistent markup; adopt a uniform citation style in line with the bibliography for presentation quality.
The general Lagrange polynomial is stated; an explicit application with four sample points here would solidify understanding.
